identity.title: Motor Vehicle Supplies and New Parts Merchant Wholesalers

lens.included_activities: Independent US warehouse distributors and jobbers that repeatedly source, stock, pick, sell, and deliver new motor-vehicle parts, supplies, accessories, tools, equipment, batteries, and automotive glass to other wholesalers, retailers, repair shops, fleets, and business users.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturer-owned or captive sales branches, commission agents and brokers, retailers, used-parts wholesalers, tire-and-tube wholesalers, shells, internal reorganizations, and businesses lacking transferable recurring customer and supplier relationships.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B customers place replenishment and repair-driven orders from stocked assortments; distributors earn product gross margin and may provide cataloging, fitment support, availability, fulfillment, delivery, returns, warranty handling, and credit.

a.bridge: BLS publishes occupation employment and wages for four-digit NAICS 423100, not a wage-weighted task study of 423120. I map portions of sales, customer service, order entry, bookkeeping, inventory records, catalog/fitment search, purchasing, pricing, and dispatch to AI exposure and leave physical driving, picking, handling, repair, and accountable negotiation largely unexposed. The broader group includes whole vehicles, tires, and used parts, so it may understate the information intensity of new-parts cataloging or overstate it if 423120 is more warehouse-heavy.
a.rationale: The 423100 proxy reports 19.66% of employment in sales and 13.83% in office/administrative support, including customer service, orders, bookkeeping, shipping records, and general office work. New-parts distribution adds highly structured SKU, compatibility, inventory, pricing, and replenishment tasks that are amenable to AI assistance. Exposure remains bounded by transportation/material-moving at 29.96%, installation/repair at 16.10%, and human responsibility for exceptions and account relationships.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task-and-time study across 20 independent 423120 warehouse distributors and jobbers, with separate measures for catalog/fitment, purchasing, inside sales, order entry, warehouse, delivery, returns, warranty, finance, and already-automated work.
caveats: OEWS covers all of NAICS 423100 rather than 423120.
Occupation employment is not wage-weighted task exposure and does not measure current automation.
Warehouse distributors, local jobbers, and specialty-equipment distributors have different labor mixes.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be feasible in part identification and fitment assistance, demand forecasting, reorder suggestions, quote and email generation, price monitoring, customer-service triage, returns classification, and invoice or order processing. BLS says online wholesale increasingly complements face-to-face selling and that AI/chatbots may limit sales employment. Fragmented supplier data, legacy ERP and catalog systems, fitment liability, service-level expectations, and warehouse exceptions prevent full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Production telemetry from distributors using AI-enabled PIM/catalog, ERP, CRM, demand-planning, and service tools, reporting labor hours, fill rates, wrong-part returns, override rates, customer adoption, and implementation cost for at least 12 months.
caveats: No public 423120 study measures implemented AI opportunity over five years.
Fitment errors can impose customer downtime and warranty costs, requiring human review.
Smaller firms may lack clean product and transaction histories or integration staff.

e.rationale: The Census form identifies recurring distribution archetypes directly: warehouse distributors selling primarily to jobbers or wholesalers and jobbers selling primarily to retailers and repair shops. Those businesses generally provide repeat replenishment, availability, fitment, delivery, and returns functions to external customers. The eligible share is reduced by manufacturer sales branches, captive operations, import or trading businesses without recurring service, customer or supplier concentration, and owner-dependent firms without a transferable organization.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of the dataset-estimated LMM population using ultimate ownership, type of operation, customer classes, repeat-order share, delivery routes, employee depth, customer concentration, and owner dependence, validated through a representative operator survey.
caveats: Public Census materials define establishment types but do not measure the eligible LMM firm share.
The broader 4231 survey indicates that manufacturer sales branches are material, but their share may differ in 423120.
The dataset n is estimated from revenue classes and an external margin bridge, not observed EBITDA.

s5.rationale: A qualifying five-year control transfer near one fifth is a bounded judgment for independent, transferable distributors. Retirement and succession, regional consolidation, supplier pressure, and ordinary private-company sales create potential supply, but many owners hold, transfer within families, sell to employees, close, or divest assets without transferring the operating platform.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed transaction data from automotive aftermarket distributors and industry-focused brokers, mapped to 423120 and deal type, combined with an owner survey on age, intended exit horizon, successor availability, and willingness to sell control.
caveats: No public source measures qualifying control transfers for 423120.
Deal databases undercount small private transactions and overrepresent sponsor-backed consolidation.
Closures and internal reorganizations must not be treated as transferable opportunities.

q.rationale: Merchant distributors sell goods on their own account and carry inventories, so they can initially retain workflow gains through lower service cost, fewer wrong-part returns, improved fill rates, tighter purchasing, and faster inventory turns rather than an explicit cost pass-through. Repeated bidding, visible online prices, buying groups, national chains, supplier programs, and customer switching should return a substantial share through lower spreads or better service. Differentiated availability, local delivery, fitment quality, and credit can preserve some benefit.
q.change_evidence: Customer-SKU gross-margin panels before and after automation, including price realization, rebates, returns, fill rates, inventory turns, delivery cost, retention, and win/loss outcomes by customer type and competitive intensity.
caveats: No observed study isolates retention of AI-generated benefit in 423120.
Inventory purchasing gains may be cyclical or vendor-driven rather than repeatable AI benefit.
Large repair chains and retailers may capture more benefit than fragmented independent customers.

d5.bridge: FHWA measures the US registered vehicle stock and vehicle miles, not wholesale demand for new parts, while BLS projects employment for broad NAICS 423100 rather than 423120 real output. I use 297.5 million registered vehicles and 3.294 trillion vehicle-miles in 2024, both above 2019 levels, as installed-base and usage signals and the BLS 4.5% ten-year employment increase as a conservative channel check. Parts demand can diverge because vehicle age, repair intensity, OEM content, prices, and distributor channel share change.
d5.rationale: New replacement parts are consumed by a large, used vehicle stock and recurring vehicle use. FHWA reports registered vehicles rising from 276.5 million in 2019 to 297.5 million in 2024 and VMT rising from 3.262 trillion to 3.294 trillion. BLS projects 423100 employment from 381,100 in 2024 to 398,400 in 2034. These proxies support modest real five-year growth, tempered by EV mix changes, durable components, direct sourcing, and cyclical fleet and repair activity.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar parts-demand forecast by vehicle class, powertrain, age cohort, and part category, tied to projected miles, failure/replacement rates, and the independent-distributor share of units.
caveats: Registered vehicles and miles are demand drivers, not wholesale parts purchases.
FHWA changed from estimated 2022-2023 registration data to actual state-reported data for 2024.
The BLS projection aggregates whole vehicles, new parts, tires, and used parts and measures employment rather than output.
Powertrain transition changes category demand: EVs remove some maintenance parts while adding different electronic, thermal, and chassis content.

o.rationale: Most year-five parts-distribution quantity should still require an accountable operator to curate assortments, hold inventory and credit risk, assure provenance and fitment, fulfill rapidly, deliver, accept returns, manage warranty claims, and resolve shortages. Software can make discovery and ordering self-service, and manufacturers or marketplaces can bypass some distributors, but it does not itself stock and move the physical part or bear service-level and fitment accountability.
o.change_evidence: Channel-share tracking for parts units ordered and fulfilled manufacturer-direct or through marketplaces without an independent distributor, plus service-level, return, warranty, and emergency-delivery data for digitally self-served accounts.
caveats: Some physical fulfillment can shift to manufacturer or third-party logistics operators rather than the current distributor.
The broad OEWS physical-labor shares may not represent 423120 precisely.
Standardized catalog data and platform guarantees could accelerate disintermediation.

narrative.executive_view: New-parts distribution has a credible recurring-customer model and a meaningful information-processing layer around a physical inventory and delivery core. AI can improve fitment search, pricing, forecasting, order handling, and service, while the industry's low but nontrivial labor intensity and competitive pass-through limit the absolute retained benefit.
narrative.ai_impact: The strongest applications are catalog and fitment assistance, quote and order automation, demand and replenishment suggestions, service triage, pricing support, invoice extraction, returns classification, and exception prioritization. Physical receiving, picking, delivery, warranty accountability, credit, and difficult fitment or availability decisions remain operator work.
narrative.value_capture: Distributors can retain savings through fewer errors and returns, lower service cost, better availability, and faster inventory turns. Online price transparency, buying power, supplier programs, and repeated customer comparison will share benefits, while fast local fulfillment, specialized assortment, credit, and reliable fitment can protect some economics.
narrative.firm_availability: Census materials identify warehouse distributors and jobbers serving wholesalers, retailers, and repair shops, which fits repeat external service. Eligibility is reduced by captive manufacturer branches and owner-dependent or concentrated businesses. The five-year control-transfer rate remains an estimate because public industry transaction data are absent.
narrative.demand_durability: A large and growing registered vehicle stock and vehicle miles support recurring replacement-parts need. Five-year real demand should be resilient but category-specific: electrification, vehicle durability, direct sourcing, and channel consolidation may displace legacy categories or intermediaries without removing the need to supply physical parts.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Public occupation and projection evidence is only available at the four-digit 423100 level, while 423120 itself spans warehouse distributors, jobbers, automotive glass and batteries, heavy-vehicle parts, tools, accessories, and petroleum-marketing equipment. Fitment errors and dirty catalogs can limit AI deployment, and inventory and supplier economics may dominate labor savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Frequent, SKU-rich replenishment and repair orders create repeatable AI-assisted search, fitment, pricing, forecasting, and service workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Direct evidence on wage-weighted task exposure, eligible independent firms, qualifying transfers, and retained AI benefit is unavailable at six-digit level.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form: Motor Vehicle Supplies and New Parts Merchant Wholesalers
sources.supports: Identifies 423120 warehouse distributors selling mainly to jobbers or wholesalers and jobbers selling mainly to retailers and repair shops; distinguishes own-account merchant wholesalers, manufacturer branches, agents, brokers, and electronic markets; asks about inventories, e-commerce, customer classes, selling methods, and technologies.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 423100
sources.supports: Reports 367,580 total jobs; 19.66% sales, 13.83% office/administrative support, 16.10% installation/maintenance/repair, and 29.96% transportation/material-moving employment, with detailed occupations and wages.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Wholesale and Manufacturing Sales Representatives
sources.supports: Describes prospecting, customer needs, pricing, negotiation, contracts, orders, follow-up, reports, and online selling; says online wholesale mostly complements face-to-face sales and AI/chatbots may limit employment growth.

sources.title: Motor Vehicle and Motor Vehicle Parts and Supplies Merchant Wholesalers: 2022
sources.supports: Defines the 4231 merchant model, reports that manufacturer sales branches represented 49.9% of 2022 group sales and independent merchant wholesalers 50.1%, and reports substantial sales, inventories, operating expenses, and payroll expense for the group.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: Projects employment in NAICS 423100 from 381,100 in 2024 to 398,400 in 2034, an increase of 17,300 or 4.5%.

sources.title: FHWA Highway Statistics 2024: Motor Vehicle Traffic Fatalities, 1900-2024
sources.supports: Reports 297,525,836 registered vehicles and 3.294 trillion vehicle-miles in 2024, compared with 276,491,174 registered vehicles and 3.262 trillion miles in 2019.

sources.title: FHWA Highway Statistics 2024: Annual Vehicle-Miles of Travel, 1980-2024
sources.supports: Reports national annual vehicle-miles rising from 3.262 trillion in 2019 to 3.294 trillion in 2024, after the 2020 disruption.
